text: To be, or not to be, that is the question: Whether 'tis nobler in the mind to suffer the slings and arrows of outrageous fortune, or to take arms against a sea of troubles, and by opposing end them.
context: Hamlet's famous meditation on life, death, and action.

text: What a piece of work is a man! How noble in reason, how infinite in faculty! In form and moving how express and admirable! In action how like an angel! In apprehension how like a god!
context: Hamlet's awe at human potential, followed by his disillusionment.

text: This above all: to thine own self be true, and it must follow, as the night the day, thou canst not then be false to any man.
context: Polonius's famous (if somewhat hypocritical) advice on integrity.

text: Once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more; or close the wall up with our English dead.
context: Henry's rousing call to his troops at the siege of Harfleur.

text: We few, we happy few, we band of brothers; for he today that sheds his blood with me shall be my brother.
speech: Henry V, Act 4 Scene 3 (St Crispin's Day Speech)
context: Henry's legendary speech before the Battle of Agincourt.

text: The game's afoot! Follow your spirit, and upon this charge cry 'God for Harry, England, and Saint George!'
context: The closing battle cry of the 'breach' speech.

text: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears; I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him.
speech: Julius Caesar, Act 3 Scene 2 (Mark Antony's Funeral Oration)
context: The beginning of Antony's masterful, ironic oration that turns the crowd.

text: The evil that men do lives after them; the good is oft interred with their bones.
context: Antony's subtle opening that undercuts his own disclaimer.

text: I am no orator, as Brutus is; but, as you know me all, a plain blunt man, that love my friend.
context: Antony's brilliant false modesty while manipulating the crowd.

text: Cry 'Havoc!' and let slip the dogs of war.
speech: Julius Caesar, Act 3 Scene 1 (Mark Antony, after Caesar's assassination)
context: Antony's private vow of vengeance immediately after the murder.

text: All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players; they have their exits and their entrances; and one man in his time plays many parts.
speech: As You Like It, Act 2 Scene 7 (Jaques)
context: Jaques's famous melancholic reflection on the seven ages of man.

text: Now is the winter of our discontent made glorious summer by this sun of York.
context: Richard's famous opening that reveals his villainy and ambition.

text: A horse! a horse! my kingdom for a horse!
context: Richard's desperate cry on Bosworth Field.

text: Out, out, brief candle! Life's but a walking shadow, a poor player that struts and frets his hour upon the stage and then is heard no more.
speech: Macbeth, Act 5 Scene 5 (Tomorrow and tomorrow and tomorrow soliloquy)
context: Macbeth's nihilistic reflection upon learning of Lady Macbeth's death.

text: Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow, creeps in this petty pace from day to day to the last syllable of recorded time.
context: The beginning of Macbeth's great speech on the meaninglessness of life.

text: If we are marked to die, we are enow to do our country loss; and if to live, the fewer men, the greater share of honour.
speech: Henry V, Act 4 Scene 3 (St Crispin's Day)
context: Henry's argument that fewer men mean greater glory for those who fight.

text: This day is called the feast of Crispian. He that outlives this day, and comes safe home, will stand a tip-toe when this day is named.
context: Promising eternal honor to those who fight on St Crispin's Day.

text: The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, but in ourselves, that we are underlings.
speech: Julius Caesar, Act 1 Scene 2 (Cassius to Brutus)
context: Cassius's argument that men make their own fate through action.

text: Cowards die many times before their deaths; the valiant never taste of death but once.
speech: Julius Caesar, Act 2 Scene 2 (Caesar to Calpurnia)
context: Caesar's famous dismissal of fear of death.

text: Men at some time are masters of their fates: the fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, but in ourselves, that we are underlings.
context: The full line emphasizing human agency over destiny.
